title: Patient-Controlled Analgesia (PCA): Safe Use
subtitle: The nurse manages a PCA pump — teaching that ONLY the patient presses the button, doing a two-nurse setup check, monitoring sedation and respirations, and keeping naloxone ready.

patient.name: Patient-Controlled Analgesia
patient.admitDate: The nurse safely manages a patient using a PCA pump for pain control.

vitals.temp: ONLY the patient presses the button

labs.v: Press when in pain, before activity.
labs.r: Do not wait for severe pain.

nursesNote: The nurse safely manages a patient-controlled analgesia (PCA) pump, which lets the patient self-administer a preset IV opioid dose by pressing a button for steadier pain control. The single most important safety rule is that ONLY the patient presses the button: 'PCA by proxy,' where a family member or nurse pushes it, can cause overdose and is not permitted, and the patient must be alert and able to understand the device. Because the opioid is a high-alert medication, two nurses perform an independent check at setup and shift change, verifying the drug, concentration, demand dose, lockout interval, and any continuous (basal) rate against the order. The lockout interval limits how often a dose can be delivered. The nurse monitors sedation level and respiratory status closely, because increasing sedation appears before respiratory depression and is the best early warning sign; a low respiratory rate or oversedation means holding doses, stimulating the patient, and being ready to give naloxone, the opioid antidote kept available. Common opioid effects such as constipation, nausea, and pruritus are managed, and the patient is taught to press the button when pain begins and before activity rather than waiting until pain is severe.
providerOrders: Teach that only the patient may press the PCA button (no PCA by proxy)
Perform an independent two-nurse check of the PCA settings
Monitor sedation level, respiratory rate, and SpO2 closely
Keep naloxone available for opioid-induced respiratory depression
Manage constipation, nausea, and pruritus from the opioid

opts.text: PCA lets the patient self-dose an IV opioid
Only the patient should press the button
The opioid is a high-alert medication
Family should press the button while the patient sleeps
A lockout interval limits how often a dose is given
Sedation and respirations must be monitored
Increasing sedation appears before respiratory depression
A second nurse independently verifies the PCA settings
Naloxone should be available for the patient
The patient must be alert enough to use the device
PCA removes the risk of opioid overdose entirely
Pressing before activity helps control pain

opts.text: Stop the PCA and stimulate the patient
Open the airway and give oxygen
Administer naloxone per protocol
Notify the provider and rapid response

Teaching the patient that only they press the button
